mnemonics.title: Internal vs External Commands
mnemonics.content: INTERNAL = Inside shell, Immediate, No fork needed. EXTERNAL = Exists as file, Executed via fork+exec, Located via PATH.

mnemonics.title: Command Combination Operators
mnemonics.content: SEMICOLON (;) = Sequential - 'First; Second = Do first, THEN second ALWAYS'. AMPERSAND-AMPERSAND (&&) = AND - 'First && Second = Do second IF first SUCCEEDS'. PIPE (|) = Pipeline - 'First | Second = Output of first BECOMES input of second'. BAR-BAR (||) = OR - 'First || Second = Do second IF first FAILS'.

mnemonics.title: Shell Expansion Order
mnemonics.content: BILLY TAPS CAR WAX - Brace, Tilde, Parameter, Command substitution, Arithmetic, Word splitting, Pathname, eXecute/Quote removal.

mnemonics.title: Exit Status Meanings
mnemonics.content: ZERO = Hero (Success), NON-ZERO = Loser (Failure). Remember: Exit code 0 is the only success code.

acronyms.expansion: Program Allocation Search Technique (or Path: List of directories to Search for executables)
acronyms.usage: Determines where shell searches for external commands like ls, grep, cat
Unique number assigned to each process running in the system
Marker indicating no more data to read from input
Default input stream (file descriptor 0), typically keyboard
Default output stream (file descriptor 1), typically terminal
Error output stream (file descriptor 2), typically terminal

visualTips.visualization: Draw a decision tree: Command entered → Is it builtin? → YES: Execute directly in shell → NO: Search PATH variable → Fork child process → Exec external command → Return result to shell.

Think of command substitution as sandwich: $(echo hello) becomes the text 'hello' which then gets inserted into the command line before execution. Like a variable but holds command output instead of text.
Visualize a pipe (|) as a physical pipe connecting two processes. Data flows from left command's STDOUT into the pipe and comes out of the right command's STDIN. The pipe acts as a temporary buffer.
Parentheses () create a 'bubble' - a separate room. Changes inside the bubble (variable assignments) don't leak out. Curly braces {} would be the same room with modifications visible.

analogies.analogy: Internal commands are like actions built into your body (breathing, blinking) - they're part of you, always available instantly. External commands are like calling a specialist (plumber, electrician) - you need to find them and they come from outside.
Think of a factory assembly line. Each worker (command) does one job and passes the result to the next worker. No worker needs to know the entire process, just their input and output.

PATH is like your phone's contact list with only executable people. When you 'call' a name (type a command), your phone searches through this list to find where that person actually lives (the executable file).

Fork is like photocopying yourself - you create an identical copy. Exec is like that copy reading a different instruction manual and becoming a different person. Together they create a new process to run a program.
Exit status is like a test result: 0 = Pass (everything worked), anything else = Fail (something went wrong). Think of 0 as a thumbs up from the command.
